speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to talk about your experiences with friends you made here or with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I did very little of it, actually. I didn't really want to discuss it very much. For many, many years, we didn't speak about it very much at all. We said we're going to make a new life, and we're going to start a new life. And although my father was very always sad that he lost all that family, and my mother too, but there was like a loss-- even though he lived in Australia and he loved it, he was still-- there was always a sadness there which I felt. I felt that as a child that had gone through such a lot with them, I knew that that was something that was sad. As where I was concerned, if anyone ever asked me about things, I used to say, oh well, my story's like any other story. A lot of people went through troubles in the war. So I used to sort of not think about it. And then a few years ago, I thought, no. I want to put this on paper. So I started thinking about it a little bit, only because I have now four grandchildren. And I thought-- I've got two lovely daughters. And I thought, for my grandchildren, I would like them to understand what their grandmother went through and that it wasn't just history, that it was something that happened and that it was something that I would never wish to happen to them. I would always want them to understand that it wasn't fiction, that it wasn't something that you read about, because it was their own grandmother that witnessed it. And I felt that I wanted to put that in a-- in some sort of a story line, which I did. I did it in-- I called it, 'The Dark Years.' And I wrote it in a fashion that a child would write, like I would remember it, how I remembered it. And I thought that way, when they're older they will understand when they read this that they can relate to it like how I saw it, and similar to what I've told you, but not quite like on these terms, I suppose, because when you're speaking to someone, it's a little bit different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is when I opened a little bit. I opened it up. And people said to me, why don't you have it published? Why didn't you do this? And I said, no. It's not for that type of thing. I just want this as a-- as a reference for the family, and that they never-- I never hope that they will ever have to experience something like that ever, ever. And that was what I did, the first time really about six years ago that I spoke about that. Do you think your experiences have affected the way you've brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No-- I think I've been overprotective, maybe a bit protective of them. Maybe not overprotective, but protective. I know that my-- my children, I always worry for them. And I always sort of see that they don't do dangerous things when they were little. And I know I have one daughter, young daughter, that is a very tomboyish daughter. My older daughter is a very sincere and lovely sort of mature girl, always was a little bit more serious, whereas my younger daughter is a very, very love life girl, and also a serious girl in lots of ways. She's no-- no less than my eldest daughter, but she was more tomboyish. And she would like to go in, for instance, horse riding and things like that. And I would say, no, I won't let you. It's too dangerous. I'm not going to. Mommy, please, it's nothing. People do all the-- No. I could already see the danger if she fell off the horse. She could break her neck. Now, this sort of things, maybe most mothers do this. I don't know. But I was very strong on that. And I said no. Mandy, please, I don't want you to do it. No-- so maybe-- and then also with-- with holidays and things, I always take care, drive carefully. I never let them out of my sight unless I say drive carefully, watch, and let me know when you get home. These are the things that I-- maybe they've rubbed off. Maybe it's normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you returned to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went about six years ago. And I did with my youngest daughter a little bit of a tour of Holland, where we and my cousin, my brother-- I call him my brother. I don't like to call him my cousin, because he is my brother really. Over all these years, he's become my brother. He's only three years younger than me. And we grew up together. He met me in Holland, and we did a nice tour of Holland like tourists. And I showed my daughter some of the hiding places, one of the houses that we stayed in, and where I was born, and the lane where these people-- these people were-- you know, where they had the coal and all this business. And I showed her. I found them all and I showed her where we were. And she said it was most enlightening. And it was sad, and it was also good to show her a little bit. But that was all. I saw my uncle and my aunt, because my uncle married [? Rita, ?] the-- the lady, the Father and Mother Visser's daughter. So that became my auntie.  It was interesting. After the war, people married each other, you know, when they'd been in hiding and things like that. They've got lovely daughters too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, that would be more or less the last time I've been to Holland. And I don't know when I'll go. I would like to see my uncle-- he's getting on in years, my father's brother-- once more before, you know, it's too late sort of thing. Is there a message that you would like to add for your children and grandchildren and other people watching this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think maybe I've already said it, because I said in-- in the previous few lines that I've spoken. I said that I wanted it-- and that is the reason why I am being interviewed today, because I've decided to speak up. And maybe it will give me a feeling of that I've got it off my shoulders. But also at the same time, I wanted for my children and my grandchildren and my great-grandchildren that they should live in peace and harmony with their fellow men, and that they should never or any child of any denomination should have to endure this kind of situation where a war will rob you of everything. And I think that is the message that I would like to give for the whole world at-- at large. Unfortunately, where it was with the Jewish people, it was slightly different. Some nations fight themselves. This was a-- a massacre and a genocide. But I hope that will never, ever happen again. But as I say, for the whole world, children should never, ever have to suffer the indignities of any kind of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add which I haven't asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I've done my best. I really think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, thank you for sharing your story with us, Corrie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Corrie, could you tell me who this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, my mother's father, Nathan Van Coevorden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been well before the war, probably 1930-something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this would have been in Amsterdam. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, Frederika Van Coevorden. That was taken round about the same time. I can't honestly give you dates here. I have no idea. That-- they were husband and wife. And they were first cousins too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rosa Van Coevorden. This was my great-grandmother, was the mother of Frederika Van Coevorden, which is my grandmother. And that's my grandfather, Nathan Van Coevorden. That is Joel Van Coevorden. That was one of the sisters of my grandmother. That's my mother, [? Judik ?] Swaab, Simon Swaab, and Rosa Van Coevorden, called Robbie. All these people were brothers and sisters of my grandmother. And they were all killed during the war. And they had children. This was a photo that-- there's a little boy here that you can see. And he had a little brother. And there were more children there, which I've got here the family tree. They all were killed in the-- in-- during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when and where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I can't tell you. It must have been a family gathering. And I have a feeling it might have been my great-grandmother's 80th birthday, because she was celebrating with her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks. Who are these people, Corrie?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Cornelia [? Appel-Swaab, ?] which was my grandmother. This is Herman Swaab. And this is me, Corrie Swaab.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken when I was about two years old, and it would have been 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Judik Swaab. This is my father, Simon Swaab. That is [? Rita ?] Swaab, which was-- he was married to my uncle, Lou Swaab. And this is me. And this was just-- this was on the [NON-ENGLISH] This was just when we were allocated that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, Cornelia Swaab. That's my mother, Judik Swaab. And this is me outside the shop of my grandfather in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 Maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Who are these people, Corrie?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Judik Swaab; my grandmother, Frederika Van Coevorden; my father, Simon Swaab; my grandfather, Nathan Van Coevorden; and [? Ute ?] Van Coevorden, who was the wife of my Uncle Joel. And that's me, Corrie Swaab.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by my age-- I think it looks Amsterdam, but where exactly I don't know. And I-- I look about three there. Can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Corrie. And I would have been-- this would have been just before I was going into hiding, about nearly four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are these three people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. This is my young brother, Frederick Swaab. And this is Harry Lezer, L-E-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Amsterdam. And I was about 13 years old, so it would have been about 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Debbie. This is my daughter Mandy. That's my husband Reuben Aaron. And this is me, Corrie Aaron. And the date was the 16th of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Sydney. We were having our wedding anniversary. It was our 41st wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother, [? Greta ?] Swaab. And that was taken on her 80th birthday in the garden of my brother's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Simon Swaab. And this was taken on the engagement of my brother Fred. And I don't-- I can't remember what year that was. We'll have to forget about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the home of-- where the engagement party was being held.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Where is this, Corrie?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Haarlem. And this is in the room of Visser [INAUDIBLE]. And this is the house where we were in hiding with Father and Mother Visser. And this is the girl, the young granddaughter that I'm standing with at the doorway. And it's a very nice story, because she decided to buy that house. And they're living there now, so I managed to go and see that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years ago. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Bergen op Zoom. And this is the place where I was born. And as you can see, the pram is sitting out in the front there. This was the shop. And there was me in the pram, and it looks like with my father. And I can't quite see whether that's the lady that used to work in the shop or not or my mother, can't see. That was up the [INAUDIBLE], Bergen op Zoom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And where's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the same place, Bergen op Zoom, of the [INAUDIBLE]. And that is that shop where I was born with the building above it. And that's me in the doorway. And I went back six years ago to see what it looked like today. And this they've made a restaurant cum coffee shop out of it. And so that's the difference between-- that was about six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. In 1942, when my grandmother was-- the house was raided with her two daughters, and my grandfather was in the hospital. They picked up my grandmother and the two girls, Lena and Liesje. And she somehow managed to get a letter-- we think she must have thrown it out of the train, or maybe they stopped somewhere and they managed to get it out. It came to us [INAUDIBLE] in Amsterdam. And it was sent to Simon Swaab, then here, Simon [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] 296. And the date on that is 1942. And the stamp is Assen, which is on the way to Westerbork. And that's where they were going. And the reason why I felt that it was important to go onto this is because she wrote here, 'Dear Simon and Greta and my darling Corrie.' And I feel that I would like to have-- and the reason she wrote is to say that they're on the way. They have nothing with them and they are wondering whether it's worthwhile for anybody to send any luggage onto them. So they're really a little bit bewildered what's going on there. And they write, 'Take care of yourself and tell Pa,' which is my grandfather who was in hospital, 'to make it to Lou,' which Lou was already in hiding. So that was the message. And that was the last thing we heard of them. And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. What is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a document to verify that my husband-- my father was in the underground and that he had served and done his work for the-- it says here, 'living in Amsterdam [INAUDIBLE] and active and bona fide illegal worker and it is [INAUDIBLE] 1940, 1945 in hiding.' I can't quite read the balance of it, but that's what it was, that he was a bona fide underground worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he obtain this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I found this in the safety deposit box.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: District Noord-HOlland. I have no idea-- Haarlem. So-- and the date is not quite finished there, so I don't know. But it's 1940, 1945, so it's somebody that has, you know, clarified this matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This family tree is only part of the family that we lost during the war. I have only got certain members. And of course, I can't get everyone. But this shows just in general what happened to one family, one section only of a family. All the asterisks are the killed in the Holocaust. And just the circles are the few that are surviving the Holocaust. And that's really all that I wanted to show here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you introduce us to these people, Corrie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband, Reuben Aaron. And this is my daughter Debbie Hauseman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm very proud of my mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was really hard for her to do this today, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, had Corrie ever spoken to you much about her story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not much really. But the time when we got married, she'd wake up and screaming. And I just didn't know what the hell she was really screaming about, you know. It was really a nightmare for her. And then slowly she started to tell me a few things, not much, really. I think I-- I learned more about what happened when she wrote her book. That was the only time really. I think she's come out all right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Debbie, how did you feel when you read your mother's story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to tell you the truth, I've never been able to read it, because over the years I've heard a lot from both my mom and my grandmother when she was alive. And I just couldn't face it. But one day I know that I will. I just haven't got to that stage yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you might be able to see the video.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Interesting reading, you know, it's very interesting reading. I've read it several times. You couldn't grasp the whole thing in one reading. You really have to read it a couple of times before you really got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's really tremendous for a girl of five. And I have a granddaughter who is five, and we were just talking about the other day as to what would happen? And she's very bright. Very bright. Yeah. Very. Would anybody like to add anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really that I can think of off-hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's just nice having them here. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's-- it's very difficult. I don't know how anybody can really block this memory. It's always going to happen. You're going to remember. But I think having the-- the children and the grandchildren, well, makes life a lot easier. So. Yeah. And we are both, I mean, all--

speaker: interviewee
text: All's worked out well in the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We got lovely--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very proud of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good family we have. We know she's been through a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK. Well, thank you for coming today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
